quote: they only do Tesla Powerwalls. And so We're probably gonna be testing. I was yeah. But I was I'm I'm you know, I I I wanted to do something like a a EG four system... with, the 48 volt batteries so that I could, expand it later with, you know, non brand specific batteries.
stewart_read: Chris is not a Tesla-sourced lead with a product expectation gap — he is an existing solar owner who has already evaluated Powerwall and rejected it in favor of a non-brand-specific battery architecture. This is a product-fit qualification moment, not a Tesla relationship reframe. Bucky does not acknowledge the non-Powerwall preference, does not flag it for the closer, and does not probe whether Ion's catalog can serve this need. The codex marks battery_storage_inquiry as a TBD cross-sell signal, meaning the correct routing path is unresolved — but the failure to capture and flag the customer's stated requirement is clear regardless.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Bucky heard the requirement and intentionally deferred product-fit discussion to the specialist. Rejected — there is no evidence of a deliberate handoff flag. Bucky's 03:58 response ('tons of batteries') suggests he did not register the constraint at all.
coaching_implication: Manager should ask Bucky: 'What did you hear Chris say he wanted, and what did you tell him Ion carries?' The gap between those two answers is the coaching moment. Separately, manager needs to verify with Kenny whether Ion's battery catalog has expanded beyond Powerwall before the 2PM appointment.

quote: Yeah. We have tons of batteries. We have, like, multiple.
stewart_read: Per the 2026-05-15 Kenny confirmation, Ion's battery offering is Powerwall-only. If that is still current, Bucky's answer is an unverified claim from a rep who does not know the catalog — not a fabrication, but a confident assertion made without product knowledge. Even if Ion has since expanded its battery line, the correct setter move is the universal escape hatch: 'That's exactly the kind of question our specialist is built to answer — let me get you on the calendar.' Bucky instead answers a closer-tier product question with unverified specificity.
alternative_hypothesis: If Ion's battery catalog has expanded since the 2026-05-15 confirmation and does include EG4 or equivalent, Bucky's answer is directionally correct and the risk is lower. Manager must verify before the 2PM sit.
coaching_implication: Two separate coaching beats: (1) product knowledge — Bucky should know what Ion actually carries before asserting it; (2) scope discipline — product catalog questions belong to the specialist regardless. The escape hatch ('our specialist will walk through all the options') protects Bucky from both gaps simultaneously.

quote: And your panels, are you in contract with somebody, or are you independent?
stewart_read: Bucky enters the orphaned flow but executes only the first step (ownership confirmed at 01:28: 'Yes. I own the panels'). The codex requires four steps: ownership, original company, panel size, and system working. Bucky never asks panel size (step 3) or — critically — whether the system is working (step 4). The system-working question is a hard DQ trigger per dq_rules. A non-working existing system is a hard disqualifier, and Bucky books the appointment without ever asking.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Bucky assessed from context (Chris is actively managing the system, discussing inverter upgrades) that the system is working and skipped the check as redundant. Rejected — the hard DQ check is procedural, not inferential. A rep cannot substitute contextual inference for the required question.
coaching_implication: Walk Bucky through the four-step orphaned flow in the 1-on-1. The system-working question is not optional — it is a hard DQ gate. Ask him: 'What happens if the closer gets on the call and the system is broken?' The answer to that question is why the check exists.

quote: I'm looking for options to just add storage.
stewart_read: Chris is not a passive form-fill — he has a defined technical need, has already evaluated one battery option (Powerwall), rejected it on architectural grounds (non-brand-specific expandability), and is actively shopping alternatives. This is a high-intent buyer signal. Bucky captures the surface need ('looking for a battery') but does not probe the depth of Chris's motivation, his timeline, or what specifically drove him to reject the Powerwall path. The verify.interest_in_solar item calls for 60+ seconds of depth on motivation — Bucky moves to utility company within seconds.
coaching_implication: The depth miss here is not fatal — Chris is already engaged — but it leaves the closer without context on why Chris rejected Powerwall. A single follow-up ('What made you want to move away from the Powerwall option?') would have surfaced the architectural reasoning and given the closer a sharper entry point.

quote: Alright. Yep. As soon as we're done here, I will I will pull up my Dominion account, and I'll get that screenshot and send it back to you.
stewart_read: Bucky gives Chris his personal phone number for bill follow-up (803-262-8164 at 03:14) and defers the booking to a callback after bill receipt. The codex flags this anti-pattern explicitly: personal phone number exposure plus a cooling-off gap between bill collection and appointment booking. Bucky partially recovers by staying on the line while Chris retrieves the bill live (04:47–05:17), which eliminates the callback gap. The personal number exposure remains.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Bucky offered the callback as a pressure-release to keep Chris on the line, then pivoted to live retrieval when Chris offered to pull it up immediately. Partially supported — the live retrieval outcome is better than a callback. But the personal number was already given before Chris offered to stay on.
coaching_implication: Flag the personal number pattern per the codex's bill_collection.stewart_automated_bill_capture anti-pattern note. The live retrieval recovery is good instinct — coach Bucky to lead with 'can you pull it up now?' before offering the callback path.

quote: I have about a dozen steps to get logged in here.
stewart_read: Chris signals friction with the bill retrieval process. Bucky waits silently through approximately 90 seconds of login navigation (04:47–05:26) without offering a verbal readback alternative or a pivot. The codex's tech_friction protocol calls for a pivot within 2 minutes of friction — Bucky is within that window but does not offer the alternative. Chris resolves it himself. The outcome is fine; the instinct to offer a fallback path is absent.
alternative_hypothesis: Chris is technically capable (he is managing a grid-tied solar system) and the friction is login-specific, not device-capability-specific. Waiting him out is reasonable here. Partially supported — but the codex pivot offer costs nothing and protects against the call dying on hold.
coaching_implication: Low priority given the outcome. Coach as a habit-building note: 'When a customer says they have a dozen steps to log in, offer the verbal readback option before they start. It takes 5 seconds and keeps the call moving if they hit a wall.'

quote: Alright. I'll text you after this to remind you. We'll call you tomorrow at 2PM, and it'll be for the battery.
stewart_read: Bucky confirms the appointment with time, channel (phone), and purpose — three of the required button_up elements. Missing: the one-hour block statement, the no-driving/at-computer anchor, and the 'is that fair?' verbal handshake. The no-driving/at-computer anchor is particularly relevant here — Chris is a technical buyer who will likely want to reference specs during the call. The 'is that fair?' handshake is a show-rate predictor the codex marks as required.
coaching_implication: Run Bucky through the full button_up checklist in the 1-on-1. The booking is real but the no-driving/computer anchor and verbal handshake are missing. For a technical buyer like Chris, the computer anchor is especially important — he will want to pull up specs.

quote: And do you want me to text you any details on what I'm looking for?
stewart_read: Chris volunteers to send additional technical details unprompted — a strong engagement signal from a buyer who wants the appointment to succeed. Bucky's response ('I don't think you got it. Any I mean, I I got them what I need, but if you wanna text any other details, it'll always help') is a missed anchor. This was an opportunity to say exactly what the specialist needs to know (the non-Powerwall requirement, the 48V architecture preference) so the closer walks in prepared. Instead Bucky deflects with vague reassurance.
coaching_implication: This is the easiest coaching win on the call. Bucky could have said: 'Yes — text me the inverter model you have now and what battery capacity you're thinking. I'll make sure the specialist has it before the call.' That one move closes the product-fit information gap and signals to Chris that his details were heard.
